title: Raw Materials, Primary Metals, and Castings
narrationScript: Every part an inspector certifies started as rock, and each process between ore and hardware leaves a signature a Level III must read. This lesson follows the metal. Ore is crushed, concentrated, and reduced — iron in the blast furnace and oxygen converter, aluminum by electrolysis, premium aerospace alloys remelted under vacuum to strip gas and inclusions. Molten steel becomes ingot or continuously cast strand, and deoxidation practice decides its flaws: killed steel solidifies quietly but takes a deep shrinkage pipe, while rimmed steel trades a clean skin for blowholes and a segregated core. Rolling the ingot into plate, bar, and tube refines grain and welds clean voids shut, but flattens pipe into laminations and draws inclusions into stringers and seams. Castings skip the working entirely: green sand molding pours into moist clay-bonded sand, permanent molds and die casting chill metal against steel, and investment casting builds ceramic shells around wax to deliver turbine-blade geometry. Process by process, fabrication chooses the discontinuity — and the discontinuity chooses the NDT method.

sections.heading: From Ore to Metal: Raw Materials Processing
sections.body:
Every aerospace part begins as rock, and the road from ore to metal sets the cleanliness — and therefore the inspectability — of everything downstream. **Raw materials processing** starts at the mine: ore is crushed and ground, then **concentrated** to separate the metal-bearing mineral from the waste rock (gangue) by froth flotation, magnetic separation, or gravity methods, and often agglomerated into sinter or pellets a furnace can digest.

**Iron and steel.** The **blast furnace** reduces iron oxide ores (hematite, magnetite) with **coke**, which serves as both fuel and the source of the carbon monoxide that strips oxygen from the ore, while **limestone** fluxes the gangue into a floating slag. The product, **pig iron**, carries roughly **3.5–4.5% carbon** plus silicon, manganese, sulfur, and phosphorus — far too dirty to use as-is. **Steelmaking** burns the excess out: the **basic oxygen furnace (BOF)** blows pure oxygen across molten pig iron and scrap, refining a heat in well under an hour, while the **electric arc furnace (EAF)** melts a charge that can be 100% scrap. **Ladle refining and vacuum degassing** then trim the final chemistry and pull out dissolved hydrogen — the gas that, left behind, can crack heavy sections from the inside (hydrogen flakes).

**Light metals.** Aluminum takes an electrochemical road: bauxite is digested into alumina by the **Bayer process**, then the **Hall–Héroult cell** electrolyzes that alumina, dissolved in molten cryolite at about 960 °C, into primary aluminum. Titanium is won by the **Kroll process** — titanium tetrachloride reduced by magnesium into porous **sponge** that must itself be melted under vacuum.

**Premium aerospace melting.** For rotating-grade alloys, air melting is not clean enough. **Vacuum induction melting (VIM)**, **electroslag remelting (ESR)**, and **vacuum arc remelting (VAR)** — often stacked as a *triple-melt* sequence — shrink the population of oxide inclusions and dissolved gas. Those inclusions are exactly what ultrasonic examination of billet hunts, so the melt route effectively writes the NDT acceptance class the material can meet.

sections.heading: Metal Ingot Production: Pipe, Gas, and the Deoxidation Bargain
sections.body:
Classically, molten steel is **teemed** into massive cast iron **ingot molds** and left to freeze. Solidification starts at the cold mold walls and marches inward as **columnar grains**, so the last liquid sits at the **top center** — and two problems concentrate there. First, metal shrinks as it solidifies; with no liquid left to feed it, the top center collapses into a funnel-shaped cavity, the **pipe**. Second, the solute elements rejected by the freezing solid — carbon, sulfur, phosphorus — accumulate in that same last liquid as **segregation**. The defense is geometric: pour big-end-up under an insulating or exothermic **hot top** that keeps a feeding reservoir liquid longest, confining the pipe and the worst segregation to a head that is **cropped** off before rolling. Crop too little, and the pipe rides into the wrought product.

Dissolved oxygen adds a third actor: as the steel freezes, oxygen reacts with carbon to form **carbon monoxide bubbles**. How completely the melt is deoxidized — its **deoxidation practice** — is a genuine bargain:

- **Killed steel** is fully deoxidized with silicon and aluminum additions. It solidifies quietly (hence "killed"), free of gas and chemically uniform — at the price of the **deepest pipe**. Forging-quality and aerospace steels are killed steels.
- **Semi-killed steel** is partially deoxidized: a controlled amount of CO evolution offsets part of the solidification shrinkage, so the pipe is smaller.
- **Rimmed steel** (practical only below roughly 0.25% carbon) is barely deoxidized. Vigorous CO evolution stirs the mold, freezing a **rim of nearly pure iron** with excellent surface — ideal for sheet — over a ring of **blowholes** and a strongly **segregated core**, with little or no pipe.
- **Capped steel** lets rimming action run briefly, then stops it: surface like rimmed, interior closer to semi-killed.

Ingots also carry **nonmetallic inclusions** — deoxidation products such as alumina and silicates, manganese sulfides, entrained slag, and eroded refractory — plus surface scabs and splash laps from teeming. **Continuous (strand) casting**, today's dominant route, pours through a tundish into a water-cooled copper mold and withdraws a solidifying strand that is cut to length: the per-ingot pipe and crop loss largely disappear, though **centerline segregation and shrinkage** remain watch items. Aluminum's equivalent is **direct-chill (DC) casting** of ingot and billet.

sections.caption: Left: a big-end-up killed ingot confines pipe and the worst segregation to a croppable hot top — and an under-cropped pipe rolls out into a centerline lamination. Right: the deoxidation bargain in cross-section — killed (quiet, uniform, deep pipe), semi-killed (smaller pipe, some gas), rimmed (clean iron rim over blowholes and a segregated core).

sections.heading: Wrought Primary Metals: Working the Ingot into Product
sections.body:
Metal becomes **wrought** the moment it is mechanically worked. The primary mill hot-rolls ingots or strand into three semifinished shapes: **slabs** (broad rectangles destined for plate, sheet, and strip), **blooms** (large squares, roughly 150 mm / 6 in. and up, for structural shapes and heavy forging stock), and **billets** (smaller squares and rounds for bar, rod, wire, and seamless tube). Finishing mills take it from there — rolling plate and sheet, drawing rod into wire, and **rotary-piercing** billets over a mandrel to make seamless tubing. Forgings and extrusions, treated with the forming processes later in this module, start from the same blooms and billets.

Hot working above the recrystallization temperature transforms the metal, not just its shape. It breaks up the coarse as-cast dendritic structure and **refines the grain**; it **welds shut** internal shrinkage and porosity whose surfaces are clean and unoxidized; and it elongates whatever survives along the working direction, producing **mechanical fibering** — directional grain flow that leaves strength and toughness lower across the grain than along it.

That last point is the inspector's map, because each ingot discontinuity becomes a characteristic wrought discontinuity:

- **Pipe and unhealed center porosity** flatten into **laminations** — planar separations at mid-thickness, parallel to a plate's surfaces. Straight-beam UT sees them unmistakably (an intermediate echo with loss of back reflection); radiography largely does not, because a lamination presents almost no thickness change along the beam.
- **Subsurface blowholes and ingot surface cracks**, their walls oxidized so they cannot weld shut, draw out into **seams** — tight, longitudinal surface discontinuities on bars and tubing, prime targets for MT and eddy current.
- **Inclusion clusters** elongate into **stringers** aligned with the rolling direction.
- Overfills and fins folded into the surface during a pass become **laps**, lying at shallow angles to the surface; excessive or too-cold reduction can rupture a bar's center into **internal bursts**.

Procurement-level ultrasonic specifications such as **AMS-STD-2154** for wrought aluminum exist precisely because these flaws are directional: scan plans and acceptance classes are written around the known geometry of wrought discontinuities.

sections.title: The process chooses the discontinuity
sections.body: Read a part's fabrication history and you have predicted its flaw catalog — and the method that finds it. Ingot pipe, gas, and inclusions, flattened and drawn by rolling, become **planar, directional** laminations, seams, and stringers: UT, MT, and ET territory, scanned with the grain flow in mind. Castings freeze their flaws in place as **volumetric, randomly oriented** shrinkage, gas, and inclusions: radiography's home ground, graded against reference radiographs. Fabrication writes the flaw catalog; the flaw catalog selects the NDT method — that is the working core of CP-105's product-technology requirement.

sections.heading: What Every Casting Shares
sections.body:
A casting is metal poured to final shape — no working, no wrought fiber. Its properties and its flaws are exactly **as solidified**, the same in every direction. All three molding families in this lesson share one anatomy: a **pattern**, oversized by a **shrinkage allowance**, forms the cavity; expendable molds split into a **cope** (top half) and **drag** (bottom half) along a **parting line**; **cores** form internal passages; a **gating system** — pouring basin, sprue, runners, gates — delivers the metal; **risers** hold reservoirs of liquid to feed shrinkage; **chills** steepen local cooling so solidification sweeps toward the feed metal instead of sealing it off.

Shrinkage happens three times. The liquid contracts as it cools; it contracts sharply again on **solidification** — the stage risers must feed; and the solid contracts to room temperature — the stage the pattern allowance covers. Starve the second stage anywhere and the casting keeps the deficit as **shrinkage cavities**, from gross piping under a riser to spongy **microshrinkage** strung between dendrite arms.

The rest of the casting discontinuity family: **gas porosity and blowholes** from dissolved gas or mold moisture; **inclusions** of sand, slag, dross, or ceramic; **hot tears** — ragged ruptures where a restrained hot spot was pulled apart while still partly liquid; **cold shuts** — smooth, unfused boundaries where two metal fronts met too cold to weld; **misruns**, where the cavity never completely filled; and **core shift**, which thins one wall while thickening its opposite. Cooling rate is the hidden property lever: the faster the freeze, the finer the grain and the better the mechanical properties — much of why mold material matters. For critical aerospace hardware, **hot isostatic pressing (HIP)** can close internal porosity that does not connect to the surface, and acceptance is organized by **AMS 2175** casting classes and grades, with radiography graded against reference radiographs such as **ASTM E155** for aluminum and magnesium.

sections.heading: Green Sand Molding
sections.body:
**Green sand molding** is the oldest, cheapest, and still the highest-tonnage casting route. Silica sand bonded with **bentonite clay and water** is rammed, jolted, or squeezed around the pattern halves in the cope and drag; "green" describes the **moist, uncured** state of the mold — like green lumber — not a color. The mold is poured once and broken apart at **shakeout**, and the sand is reconditioned and reused. Its strengths explain its reach: nearly any metal, ferrous or nonferrous; sizes from ounces to many tons; quantities from a single piece to millions; modest pattern and tooling cost.

The trade-offs are written on the part. Sand gives the **roughest as-cast surface and the widest tolerances** of the three processes, and its low thermal conductivity means **slow cooling and relatively coarse grain**. The mold's moisture is a built-in gas source: steam generated at the metal interface drives **blowholes and pinhole porosity**. Loose sand washed into the cavity becomes **sand inclusions**; steam-driven expansion of the mold face can raise **scabs**; soft ramming lets mold walls move under metal pressure; misalignment of cope and drag produces **shift** at the parting line. Internal quality is verified volumetrically — radiography against the applicable reference-radiograph grades — while surface examinations (PT on nonferrous alloys, MT on ferrous) must contend with the rough as-cast texture, which is why critical surfaces are often machined or blasted before final inspection.

sections.heading: Metal Molds: Permanent Mold and Die Casting
sections.body:
**Metal molded** casting replaces expendable sand with a reusable metal mold — and a metal mold is also a **chill**, so the casting freezes faster, with **finer grain, better mechanical properties, smoother finish, and tighter tolerances** than sand can deliver.

- **Permanent mold (gravity die) casting** pours by gravity into a coated gray iron or steel mold, hinged to open for extraction. It serves mostly **aluminum and magnesium** — plus some copper alloys and gray iron — at moderate-to-high volumes. Retractable metal cores handle simple internal passages; where geometry demands more, sand cores are set into the metal mold (**semipermanent molding**). Watch items: shrinkage at heavy sections a rigid, unyielding mold cannot feed, and cold shuts or misruns in thin walls as the rapidly chilled metal loses fluidity.
- **Die casting** injects molten metal into water-cooled steel dies at pressures on the order of **10–100 MPa (1,500–15,000 psi)** — **hot-chamber** machines for zinc and magnesium, **cold-chamber** machines for aluminum. It buys thin walls, crisp detail, excellent finish, and very high production rates. The cost hides inside: the violent, turbulent fill **entraps air and gas as dispersed internal porosity** beneath a sound, rapidly chilled skin. That porosity is why die castings are generally **neither welded nor solution heat-treated** — reheating lets the trapped gas expand and blister the part — and why die-casting design leans on the integrity of the skin.
- **Centrifugal casting**, usually in water-cooled metal molds, spins the mold about its axis so dense, sound metal packs the outer wall while lighter slag, dross, and gas migrate to the bore — stock that is simply machined away on seamless tubes and cylinder liners.

sections.caption: The casting process comparison chart. Green sand: expendable moist clay-bonded mold, any alloy and size, roughest finish. Metal molds: reusable chilled cavities — permanent mold by gravity, die casting under pressure — finer grain but trapped-gas risk. Investment: expendable wax pattern inside an expendable ceramic shell, near-net superalloy shapes with the best finish.
Three molding families side by side — what forms the cavity, what they cast, and what an inspector should expect to find.
rows: Green sand
Expendable moist clay-bonded sand mold; reusable pattern; mold destroyed at shakeout
Ferrous and nonferrous; ounces to many tons; any quantity; lowest cost
Roughest surface, widest tolerances; slow cooling gives coarser grain
Moisture-driven gas porosity, sand inclusions, shrinkage, hot tears, cold shuts, shift
Permanent mold (gravity)
Reusable coated metal mold; metal or sand cores
Mostly aluminum and magnesium; moderate-to-high volume
Better finish than sand; chilling refines grain and improves properties
Shrinkage at heavy sections, cold shuts and misruns in thin walls
Die casting (pressure)
Reusable water-cooled steel dies; metal injected at high pressure
Low-melting nonferrous: zinc, aluminum, magnesium; very high volume, thin walls
Excellent finish and detail; sound chilled skin over the core
Dispersed internal gas porosity from turbulent fill — generally not welded or solution heat-treated
Investment (lost wax)
Expendable wax pattern in an expendable fired ceramic shell
Any alloy including superalloys; near-net aerospace shapes, blades and vanes
Best as-cast finish (about 1.6–3.2 µm RMS) and tightest tolerances
Shrinkage where feeding is limited, ceramic shell or core inclusions, core shift

sections.heading: Investment Casting: The Lost-Wax Route to Aerospace Shapes
sections.body:
**Investment casting** — the lost-wax process — buys what no other route offers: near-net, intricate shapes in **any pourable alloy, including superalloys impractical to machine**, with as-cast finishes around **1.6–3.2 µm (63–125 µin.) RMS** and tolerances near **±0.13 mm per 25 mm (±0.005 in./in.)**.

The sequence: wax **patterns** are injection-molded and assembled onto a wax sprue into a **tree**. The tree is dipped into ceramic **slurry** and stuccoed with refractory grain, layer after layer, until a shell several millimeters thick surrounds every pattern. The wax is melted out in a steam autoclave or flash furnace (**dewaxing**), the shell is **fired** to cure it and burn out residue, and metal is poured into the still-hot, **preheated** shell so thin sections fill before they freeze. The shell is then broken away and the castings are cut from the tree. The name comes from "investing" — surrounding — the pattern; the older solid-mold variant encased the tree in a full block of refractory investment.

This is the route to **turbine blades and vanes**: leachable **ceramic cores** form serpentine internal cooling passages; the pour is made under **vacuum** for reactive nickel- and cobalt-base superalloys; and controlled withdrawal of the mold from the furnace hot zone yields **directionally solidified** columnar structures — or, through a grain-selector spiral, **single-crystal** blades with no grain boundaries at all. Inspection follows the criticality: **fluorescent penetrant** for the surface, **radiography graded against ASTM E192** — the reference radiographs for aerospace investment steel castings — for the interior, plus wall-thickness verification wherever a core could have shifted. The characteristic discontinuities are the process's own signature: **shrinkage** in sections the limited feeding paths starve, **ceramic inclusions** from shell or core spall, and **core shift** thinning a cooling-passage wall.

figures.title: Ingot solidification, pipe, and deoxidation practice

checkQuestions.question: Compared with a rimmed steel ingot, a fully killed ingot:
options: is completely deoxidized, solidifies quietly with uniform chemistry, and pays for it with a deep shrinkage pipe that a hot top and cropping must manage
evolves carbon monoxide vigorously, freezing a rim of nearly pure iron around a strongly segregated core
contains more dissolved gas, because silicon and aluminum additions add oxygen to the melt
requires no cropping, because full deoxidation eliminates solidification shrinkage
checkQuestions.explanation: Killing the steel with silicon and aluminum removes dissolved oxygen, so no CO evolves: the ingot freezes quietly, gas-free and chemically uniform — which is why forging-quality and aerospace steels are killed. But with no gas evolution to offset solidification shrinkage, the killed ingot takes the deepest pipe, managed by a hot top and cropping. The pure-iron rim over a segregated core describes the rimmed ingot, and deoxidation does nothing to stop shrinkage — metal contracts on freezing regardless.

checkQuestions.question: An ingot's pipe was not completely cropped before rolling into plate. The resulting discontinuity in the plate is best described as:
options: a cluster of spherical gas pores distributed through the thickness
a centerline lamination — a planar separation parallel to the plate surfaces, readily found by straight-beam UT but poorly shown by radiography
a hot tear running along the rolled edges
a transverse surface crack best detected by magnetic particle testing
checkQuestions.explanation: Rolling flattens the unfused cavity into a thin planar separation at mid-thickness, parallel to the surfaces. Straight-beam UT shows it as an intermediate echo with loss of back reflection, while radiography struggles because the lamination presents almost no thickness change along the beam. Hot tears are casting discontinuities, and a lamination is internal and planar — not a transverse surface crack.

checkQuestions.question: Hot working a cast ingot into wrought bar can be expected to:
options: remove every discontinuity the ingot contained
make the bar's properties identical in all directions
refine the coarse as-cast grain and weld clean internal voids shut, while drawing surviving inclusions into longitudinal stringers and oxidized surface defects into seams
coarsen the grain, because deformation heats the metal above its melting point
checkQuestions.explanation: Hot working above the recrystallization temperature refines the dendritic as-cast structure and can weld shut voids whose surfaces are clean — but oxidized surfaces (old blowholes and ingot surface cracks) cannot fuse and draw out into seams, while inclusions elongate into stringers. The result is mechanical fibering: properties are directional, lower across the grain flow than along it — the opposite of isotropic.

checkQuestions.question: Aluminum die castings are generally neither welded nor solution heat-treated because:
options: aluminum alloys cannot tolerate any heating after casting
the steel dies contaminate the casting surface with iron
their walls are too thin to support a weld bead
the turbulent high-pressure fill entraps gas as dispersed internal porosity, which expands and blisters the casting when it is reheated
checkQuestions.explanation: Injection at pressures on the order of 10–100 MPa fills the die violently, entraining air and gas that freeze in place as dispersed porosity beneath the sound chilled skin. Welding or solution heat treatment reheats the part enough for that trapped gas to expand and blister it. The limitation is the porosity, not aluminum chemistry, die contact, or wall thickness.

checkQuestions.question: Investment casting is the process of choice for turbine blades primarily because it:
options: delivers near-net, intricate shapes — including cored internal cooling passages — in superalloys impractical to machine, with excellent as-cast finish and tolerance
is the least expensive casting process per part
guarantees castings completely free of shrinkage and inclusions
produces the coarsest random grain structure, which is strongest at turbine temperatures
checkQuestions.explanation: A wax pattern inside a fired ceramic shell reproduces geometry no machine tool reaches — leachable ceramic cores form the serpentine cooling passages — in vacuum-poured nickel- and cobalt-base superalloys, and controlled solidification yields directionally solidified or single-crystal structures, which are engineered, not random. The process is among the most expensive per part, and it still produces its own signature flaws — shrinkage in starved sections, ceramic inclusions, core shift — which is why FPI and radiography to ASTM E192 grades remain mandatory.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024, Basic Examination Topical Outline §3.2.1–3.2.2.2 (raw materials processing; primary metals: metal ingot production and wrought primary metals; castings: green sand, metal, and investment molded)
SAE AMS 2175, Castings, Classification and Inspection of (casting classes and grades; NDT method assignment for aerospace castings)
ASTM E155, Standard Reference Radiographs for Inspection of Aluminum and Magnesium Castings
ASTM E192, Standard Reference Radiographs of Investment Steel Castings for Aerospace Applications
SAE AMS-STD-2154, Inspection, Ultrasonic, Wrought Metals, Process for (ultrasonic acceptance classes for wrought products)
